messages.content:
A container class is a data type that is capable of holding a collection of items and provides
functions to access them. Bag is an example of a container class. It is an unordered collection of
items that may have duplicates.
In this assignment, you are asked to design and develop an abstract Bag class, called
BagInterface, with the following fixed collection of operations:
Insert an item of any type into a bag
Query the bag contents: two queries
Is an item in the bag?
How many copies of an item is in the bag?
Remove an item from the bag
clear the bag
Get the size of the bag
How many items are there in the bag?
Check if the bag is empty
Check if the bag is full
Assume the bag capacity is 20
In addition to BagInterface, you are asked to implement two classes of bag
implementations: PlainBag and MagicChangeBag.

PlainBag is a simple container that holds items of any type using an array implementation.

MagicChangeBag is also very similar to PlainBag, with two exceptions. When an item is inserted into a magic change bag, it’ll magically disappear, and bag looks empty. Whenever a remove operation is invoked, all the items, except the one is removed will appear.

Certainly! Here is the UML class diagram for the BagInterface, PlainBag, and MagicChangeBag classes:

```
 _________________________       _______________________      _______________________
|        BagInterface      |     |       PlainBag        |    |    MagicChangeBag     |
|-------------------------|     |-----------------------|    |-----------------------|
| - bag: vector<T>        |     | - bag: vector<T>      |    | - bag: vector<T>      |
|-------------------------|     |-----------------------|    |-----------------------|
| + insert(item: T): void |     | + insert(item: T): void|    | + insert(item: T):void|
| + contains(item: T):bool|     | + contains(item: T):bool|    | + contains(item: T):bool|
| + count(item: T): int   |     | + count(item: T): int  |    | + count(item: T): int  |
| + remove(item: T): void |     | + remove(item: T): void|    | + remove(item: T): void|
| + clear(): void         |     | + clear(): void        |    | + clear(): void        |
| + size(): int           |     | + size(): int          |    | + size(): int          |
| + is_empty(): bool      |     | + is_empty(): bool     |    | + is_empty(): bool     |
| + is_full(): bool       |     | + is_full(): bool      |    | + is_full(): bool      |
 -------------------------       -----------------------      -----------------------
```

In the above diagram, the BagInterface class is an abstract class that defines the common operations of a bag. PlainBag and MagicChangeBag are concrete classes that implement the BagInterface. Both classes use a vector named "bag" to store the items.

The "+" symbol denotes public methods, while the "-" symbol denotes private members.

I hope this helps! Let me know if you have any further questions.

Certainly! Here is an updated version of the UML class diagram with the operations included:

```
 __________________________   _______________________    ________________________
|        BagInterface       | |       PlainBag        |  |    MagicChangeBag      |
|--------------------------| |-----------------------|  |------------------------|
| - bag: vector<T>         | | - bag: vector<T>      |  | - bag: vector<T>       |
|--------------------------| |-----------------------|  |------------------------|
| + insert(item: T): void  | | + insert(item: T): void|  | + insert(item: T): void |
| + contains(item: T): bool| | + contains(item: T):bool| | + contains(item: T):bool|
| + count(item: T): int    | | + count(item: T): int  | | + count(item: T): int   |
| + remove(item: T): void  | | + remove(item: T): void|  | + remove(item: T): void |
| + clear(): void          | | + clear(): void        |  | + clear(): void         |
| + size(): int            | | + size(): int          |  | + size(): int           |
| + is_empty(): bool       | | + is_empty(): bool     |  | + is_empty(): bool      |
| + is_full(): bool        | | + is_full(): bool      |  | + is_full(): bool       |
 --------------------------   -----------------------    ------------------------
```

In this updated diagram, I have added the operation signatures for each method. The "+" symbol indicates public access, and the "-" symbol indicates private access.

I hope this meets your requirements! Let me know if you have any further questions or need additional assistance.

Apologies for the oversight. In a UML class diagram, we can represent the relationships between classes using arrows. Here is the updated UML class diagram with the relationships:

```
 _________________________                _______________________                    _______________________
|        BagInterface      |              |       PlainBag        |                  |    MagicChangeBag     |
|-------------------------|              |-----------------------|                  |-----------------------|
| - bag: vector<T>        |              | - bag: vector<T>      |                  | - bag: vector<T>      |
|-------------------------|              |-----------------------|                  |-----------------------|
| + insert(item: T): void |  <<implements>>  | + insert(item: T): void|  <<implements>>   | + insert(item: T):void|
| + contains(item: T):bool|  ---------------->| + contains(item: T):bool|  ------------------>| + contains(item: T):bool|
| + count(item: T): int   |              | + count(item: T): int  |                  | + count(item: T): int  |
| + remove(item: T): void |  ---------------->| + remove(item: T): void|  ------------------>| + remove(item: T): void|
| + clear(): void         |              | + clear(): void        |                  | + clear(): void        |
| + size(): int           |              | + size(): int          |                  | + size(): int          |
| + is_empty(): bool      |              | + is_empty(): bool     |                  | + is_empty(): bool     |
| + is_full(): bool       |              | + is_full(): bool      |                  | + is_full(): bool      |
 -------------------------                -----------------------                    -----------------------
```

In the updated diagram, the `BagInterface` class "implements" the methods defined in the interface. The arrows indicate this relationship. The `PlainBag` and `MagicChangeBag` classes both implement the `BagInterface`, as denoted by "<<implements>>" above the arrows.

I apologize for any confusion caused. Let me know if you have any further questions or need additional assistance.

The `bag` member variable represents the collection of items in the bag. It is implemented using a `vector<T>` data structure. 

In C++, `vector` is a container that allows dynamic resizing and efficient random access to elements. In this case, it is used to store the items in the bag. The template parameter `T` represents the type of the items that can be stored in the bag.

So, `bag` is a private member variable of type `vector<T>` that holds the items in the bag.
can you do it in text base

Certainly! Here's the updated class diagram for BagInterface, PlainBag, and MagicChangeBag in text-based form:

```
_________________________________________
|              BagInterface              |
|----------------------------------------|
| - bag: vector<T>                       |
|----------------------------------------|
| + insert(item: T): void                 |
| + contains(item: T): bool               |
| + count(item: T): int                   |
| + remove(item: T): void                 |
| + clear(): void                         |
| + size(): int                           |
| + is_empty(): bool                      |
| + is_full(): bool                       |
 ----------------------------------------
          ^                 ^                ^
          |                 |                |
          |                 |                |
.vector<T>|             .vector<T>|        .vector<T>|
          |                 |                |
          .                 .                .
       PlainBag            MagicChangeBag
```

In this text-based class diagram, the lines and whitespace are used to represent the relationships between the classes. "^" represents the implementation of the BagInterface by the PlainBag and MagicChangeBag classes. ".vector<T>" indicates the type of the bag member variable, which is a vector of T type.

I hope this text-based representation is helpful! Let me know if you have any further questions.
